frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Beta, padhne-likhne mein achchi hai to ghar ke paas jo ho sake woh kara do, par roz itni door akeli bus se bhejna theek nahi lagta. Aaj kal zamaana bahut kharab hai, aur ladki ko zyada chhoot mil jaaye to ghar-grihasthi se mann hat jaata hai. Maths achchi baat hai, lekin aakhir usko ghar bhi sambhalna hai, itni door ki coaching ke liye itna jokhim kya lena.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment is misogynistic because it treats the girl's education as conditional and less important than her future domestic role, while using safety and propriety to restrict her mobility. It encodes the frame of female education as a limited privilege that should not interfere with traditional expectations.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 16 is exceptionally good at mathematics. Her school has recommended she be enrolled in a special coaching programme in another part of the city that would require her to travel by bus alone. Your son and daughter in law are considering it. You are sitting with your son in the evening after the daughter in law has raised the topic. You are telling your son what you think and why you are hesitant and what can happen to a girl who starts travelling alone at 16 and whether the mathematics is worth it.
